turns.content: I'm onboarding to this new codebase and I'm really stuck. I tried implementing the Serde trait for a custom struct but I keep getting serialization errors even though my logic seems correct. Here is what I have so far: impl Serialize for MyStruct { fn serialize<S>(&self, serializer: S) -> Result<S::Ok> where S : Serializer { let mut map = Map::new(); map.insert(key, value); serializer.serialize_map(Some(2))?;

Hey there. It sounds frustrating when the compiler won't let you pass a struct through serde_json or another crate even after you've written what looks like valid code. The issue is likely that you're trying to serialize into an internal type like HashMap or BTreeMap without explicitly handling the Map serializer interface. You need to create the map using serializer.serialize_map with the correct length, then iterate over your fields and call serialize_value for each one before closing the map with serializer.end(). This ensures the data flows through the serializer's internal state correctly.

Ah okay so I need to use serialize_map but I'm not sure about the signature. Also, what if my struct has a list of values instead of key-value pairs? Do I still just call serialize_value for each item?

For a map-like structure you definitely use serialize_map with the expected size, then iterate and call serialize_value on every entry. If your struct contains a sequence of items like a vector or tuple instead of key-value pairs, you should use serialize_seq instead of serialize_map. You create the sequence using serializer.serialize_seq(Some(count)) where count is the number of elements, then loop through your data calling serialize_value for each element before finishing with serializer.end(). The distinction matters because map expects ordered key-value pairs while seq only holds values.
That makes sense. But I'm still getting errors about lifetime parameters and the Serializer trait bounds. It seems like my struct doesn't implement Serialize properly even though all my fields do.

The problem is likely that your custom type isn't implementing the Serialize trait itself, so you can't call serialize_value on its instances inside your serializer implementation. You need to add an impl block for your specific struct with the correct lifetime annotations and the Serializer bound. Make sure every field in your struct either implements Serialize or has a dedicated impl block handling it. Also verify that you're not accidentally using older versions of serde crates where trait signatures changed, as this often breaks manual implementations.
Wait, I thought the derive macro should handle all of this automatically? Why am I manually writing out these lifetime annotations and bounds when I could just use #[serde]?

Derive macros are great for simple structs with standard library types, but they can't handle custom serialization logic like conditional formatting or nested data structures that require special handling. When you need to implement custom behavior—like skipping fields based on a flag or using a different order than the struct definition—you must manually write the Serialize implementation. The derive macro won't generate code for complex logic, so manual implementation with correct lifetime annotations and trait bounds is necessary in those cases.
